startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 52-year-old woman presents with a lump in her right breast. Work through this case exploring pectoral muscle anatomy, breast lymphatic drainage, and nerve supply.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified pectoral anatomy, breast lymphatic drainage, and nerve supply relevant to mastectomy surgery.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review pectoralis major insertion, pectoralis minor, and breast lymphatic drainage.
Revisit the three heads of pectoralis major, its insertion, nerve supply, and the predominant lymphatic drainage route of the breast.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 52-year-old woman presents with a 3-cm firm, non-tender lump in the upper outer quadrant of her right breast. There is skin dimpling and peau d'orange. Axillary lymph nodes are palpable.

You consider the anatomy of the pectoral region. Pectoralis major has three heads of origin: the clavicular head, the sternocostal head, and the abdominal head. All three heads converge to insert on the lateral lip of the bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) of the humerus.

Deep to pectoralis major lies pectoralis minor, which inserts on the coracoid process of the scapula.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient has palpable axillary lymph nodes. What is the most common route of lymphatic drainage of the breast, and what percentage does it carry?

alternatives.text: Axillary lymph nodes — receiving about 75% of breast lymphatic drainage
Internal mammary (parasternal) nodes — receiving about 75% of drainage

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Internal mammary nodes receive some drainage (especially from medial quadrants), but the dominant route is via the axillary lymph nodes, which receive approximately 75% of breast drainage.

alternatives.text: Supraclavicular nodes — receiving about 75% of drainage

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Supraclavicular nodes are involved in advanced disease. The most common route of breast lymphatic drainage is to the axillary lymph nodes (~75%).

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Approximately 75% of breast lymph drains to the axillary nodes.

The patient is scheduled for modified radical mastectomy. The surgeon must carefully preserve the nerve supply to the pectoral muscles:

  • The clavicular head of pectoralis major is supplied by the lateral pectoral nerve
  • The sternocostal head receives supply from both the lateral and medial pectoral nerves

During axillary dissection, care is also taken to preserve the long thoracic nerve (to serratus anterior) and the thoracodorsal nerve (to latissimus dorsi).

branchingQuestion.question: During surgery, the surgeon identifies pectoralis minor deep to pectoralis major. Where does pectoralis minor insert?

alternatives.text: The coracoid process of the scapula
The lateral lip of the bicipital groove of the humerus

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) is the insertion of pectoralis major, not minor. Pectoralis minor inserts on the coracoid process of the scapula.

alternatives.text: The acromion process of the scapula

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The acromion is the insertion point for parts of the deltoid and trapezius. Pectoralis minor inserts on the coracoid process of the scapula.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key pectoral region anatomy:

  • Pectoralis major: 3 heads (clavicular, sternocostal, abdominal) → inserts on lateral lip of bicipital groove
  • Pectoralis minor: Deep to pec major → inserts on coracoid process
  • Nerve supply: Lateral pectoral nerve (clavicular head), both lateral and medial pectoral nerves (sternocostal)
  • Breast lymphatics: ~75% drain to axillary nodes
